subject: Science
chapter: The World of Metals and Non-metals

key_points: The Reactivity Series is an arrangement of metals in decreasing order of their chemical reactivity.
More reactive metals (K, Na, Ca) react with cold water, while less reactive ones (Mg, Al, Zn, Fe) react only with steam.
Noble metals like Gold and Platinum are chemically inert and do not react with water, air, or acids, making them ideal for jewelry.
Corrosion of Copper results in a green coating called 'Basic Copper Carbonate', which is a mixture of $Cu(OH)_2$ and $CuCO_3$.
Anodizing and Alloying (mixing metals with other elements) are advanced methods to enhance the physical properties and corrosion resistance of materials.
examples: Alloys: Brass (Copper + Zinc), Bronze (Copper + Tin), Stainless Steel (Iron + Carbon + Chromium + Nickel)
Non-metal properties: Carbon in the form of Diamond is the hardest natural substance known.
formulas: Reactivity series displacement: $Zn(s) + CuSO_4(aq) ightarrow ZnSO_4(aq) + Cu(s)$ (Zinc is more reactive than Copper)
Formation of Basic Copper Carbonate: $2Cu + H_2O + CO_2 + O_2 ightarrow Cu(OH)_2 \cdot CuCO_3$
Hydrogen Gas Test: $2H_2 + O_2 ightarrow 2H_2O + Energy$ (The 'Pop' sound explosion)
exam_tips: Notice the state symbols ($s, l, g, aq$) in equations to show if a metal is solid or a solution.
Distinguish between 'Rusting' (specific to Iron) and 'Corrosion' (general for all metals).
Explain why Copper cannot displace Iron from Iron Sulphate ($FeSO_4$)—refer to the Reactivity Series position.
Understand that while most non-metal oxides are acidic ($SO_2$), some like Carbon Monoxide ($CO$) and Water ($H_2O$) are neutral.
